posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
In connection with the SQN-thread:

How many bottles of Chateau Rayas are in your cellar (incl. Pignan and Fonsalette - but NOT Chateau des Tours) ?

No poll, just post the number(s) … and maybe vintages …

(will be back only in 5 days)
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Precisely 1 ('00 CNdP). It’s not easy to get here…

posts.name: Mark C Johnson
posts.cooked: 26 total. 2 Pignan, 5 Rayas , the rest Fonsalette with about 1/4 of those syrah. Various vintages mostly from 2003-2005. Can’t find them/afford them now!

posts.name: Sanjay Nandurkar
posts.cooked: Only 2 bottles of Rayas reserve. I wish I had lots.

posts.name: paul hanna
posts.cooked:
About 2 dozen Rayas Reserve, back to 1979.

Had more, but I tend to drink these regularly as they are sooooo good.

posts.name: Soren R Nielsen
posts.cooked: Fonsalette '95,'96,and '96 Syrah. 1 bt. each.

posts.name: Nicos Neocleous
posts.cooked:
Russell Faulkner:

0

Russell, you don’t need to own any Rayas. Fancy trying a bottle one day?

posts.name: Stephen Williams
posts.cooked: 190, including a 1955 Vin Liquoureux

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked: Zero. Have enjoyed the few I have tried but for me they are not worth the price. Too many other wines I would rather have. I should note that I have zero Chateauneuffs in my cellar.

posts.name: brodie thomson
posts.cooked:
Rauno E (NZ):

Precisely 1 ('00 CNdP). It’s not easy to get here…

Hey Rauno, I have twice as many as you (2!). Same issue, hard to get in NZ

Brodie

posts.name: Jeff V o g e l
posts.cooked: Also 0. Zilch. Not for lack of desire to have some, but for the stark realization that many others produce decidedly gorgeous Grenache from these beautiful eastern sector sandy-clay soils for a much cheaper tariff. Giraud’s Grenaches de Pierre, Barroche’s Pure, Cristia’s Vieilles Vignes, and not a few others come to mind. Having said all that, I’d say many of these fall into a somewhat modernist vein–a nice SQN foil, perhaps??–whereas the allure of Rayas (IMHO) hinges on an arch-traditionalist’s legacy, ala Bonneau/J.P. Daumen/et al, allied to the grace of Grenache before GW and the seismic impact of Monsieur Parkér.
posts.display_username: Jeff V o g e l

posts.name: Markus S
posts.cooked: Zero. Just too damn expensive.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: 1. Which is really not enough.

posts.name: Rob Sutherland
posts.cooked:
I found a cache of Fonsalette a little while ago which I scooped up but drank my last bottle just over a month ago.

Love the stuff. What I try to open when people ask about purity in wine.

I thought I had a Pignan in the cellar but just checked CT and looks like I drank it…

Never had or bought the full fledged Rayas. It’s certainly on my bucket list.

posts.name: J a y H a c k
posts.cooked: 1. I have a lot more SQN. Rayas costs more, after all.

posts.name: Frank Drew
posts.cooked:
Jay,

I had the 2001 last winter and it was probably one of my top three wines ever. Really astonishingly good. Got it from Acker retail, I think, back in the mid-2000s.

But I only have a single bottle, the 1999. I wish prices were more in line with my comfort level.
